title: Looping Arrays 🔁
description: Combine loops and arrays for massive power.

slides.title: A Match Made in Heaven 🤝
slides.content:
Arrays and Loops are the peanut butter and jelly of programming.

Instead of writing `printf` 100 times to print out 100 elements in an array, you just write one single `for` loop that cycles through every index from `0` to `99`!

slides.title: Looping Through
slides.content: Because the loop tracker `i` naturally goes 0, 1, 2, 3... we can use `i` directly as the index inside the brackets!

quizData.question: In the previous example, why did the loop condition use `< 5` instead of `<= 5`?

options.text: If it checked `< 5`, it stops at 4. If it checked `<= 5`, it would try to access index 5, which doesn't exist and would crash!

quizData.explanation: Attempting to access `grades[5]` would look for memory OUTSIDE of the array's boundaries. This is called an 'Out of Bounds' error and is incredibly dangerous in C.

slides.title: The Out of Bounds Danger 💣
slides.content:
In C, the compiler assumes you know exactly what you are doing.

If you have a 3-item array `[0,1,2]`, and you try to forcefully print index `[1000]`, C will NOT stop you!

It will cheerfully march to that random location in RAM and print whatever random system garbage calculation happens to be living there at the moment. This is how major security hacks happen.

quizData.question: The C compiler automatically prevents you from accidentally reading past the end of an array.
quizData.explanation: C gives you raw power and assumes you are being responsible. It will blindly follow instructions to read out of bounds memory, often causing crashes or bizarre bugs.
